metadata.summary: GitOps fundamentals: Git as single source of truth, four OpenGitOps principles, push vs pull models, tool selection (ArgoCD vs Flux), and phased adoption path.

content.title: GitOps Core Principles
content.summary: GitOps uses Git repositories as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and applications. All cluster state is described in Git, applied by an in-cluster operator (pull model), and continuously reconciled — eliminating manual deployments, configuration drift, and unreliable releases.

why.p: Manual deployments cause inconsistency, configuration drift, and unreliable releases. GitOps solves this by treating Git as the authoritative record of desired state: every change is reviewed, versioned, and auditable. Pull-based reconcilers (ArgoCD, Flux) detect drift and self-heal without exposing cluster credentials to external CI systems.

Industry adoption as of 2025-2026: 90% of Kubernetes deployments use GitOps; 93% of organizations plan to continue or increase usage; 80%+ report higher reliability. ArgoCD holds ~60% market share; Flux ~25%.

list.item: Migrating a Kubernetes (or Helm/Kustomize-managed) workload off kubectl apply and CI-driven pushes onto a pull-based reconciler (ArgoCD or Flux).
Multi-cluster fleet management — single source of truth for 10+ clusters with environment promotion via PRs.
Compliance / audit-heavy environments where every cluster change must be traceable to a Git commit and reviewer.
Drift detection and self-healing of cluster state.
Teams wanting to separate CI (build/test) from CD (deploy) cleanly.
Non-declarative workloads (one-off scripts, ad-hoc data jobs) — use a CI pipeline, not GitOps.
Single small cluster with one developer and no compliance pressure — overhead exceeds benefit.
Stateful migrations with manual data steps — GitOps tools won't orchestrate "drain, snapshot, restore" sequencing; use a custom operator or runbook.
Tight imperative sequencing (deploy A, wait for hook, then B, then C) where push-based pipelines are simpler.

section.name: opengitops-principles
section.p: The four OpenGitOps principles define what qualifies as GitOps:

rule.statement: Infrastructure MUST be described declaratively (YAML/HCL), not via imperative scripts.
rule.rationale: Declarative state can be diffed, versioned, and reconciled. Imperative scripts are side-effectful and not idempotent.

rule.statement: All configuration MUST live in Git with complete history and audit trail.
rule.rationale: Git provides versioning, review workflow, and rollback. "Who changed what and why" is answered by commit history alone.

rule.statement: Changes MUST be pulled by an in-cluster agent, not pushed by CI to the cluster.
rule.rationale: Pull model eliminates credential distribution to external systems; the cluster only makes egress connections to Git.

rule.statement: The system MUST continuously reconcile actual state to desired state and detect drift automatically.
rule.rationale: Without continuous reconciliation, manual kubectl or helm operations silently diverge from Git, defeating the audit trail.

section.name: push-vs-pull
section.p: Two deployment models exist. Most 2025-2026 production setups use pull-based or hybrid:

list.item: Pull-based: GitOps operator in-cluster polls Git for changes. Best for security, multi-cluster, self-healing. Advantages: egress-only connections (no cluster exposure), no credentials distributed to external services, self-healing drift correction, better compliance and auditability.

Push-based: CI/CD pipeline pushes changes to cluster. Best for tight control, sequencing, existing pipelines. Advantages: works with existing CI/CD pipelines, better for non-Kubernetes environments, simpler initial setup, tight deployment sequencing control.
Hybrid (2025-2026): Push for velocity, pull for safety and drift correction. Used in enterprise environments needing both.

section.name: tool-selection
section.p: ArgoCD vs Flux decision matrix for 2025-2026:

list.item: UI/UX: ArgoCD has built-in web UI; Flux is CLI-first with external UI options.
Learning curve: ArgoCD lower; Flux higher.
Resource usage: ArgoCD higher; Flux lower.
Multi-tenancy: ArgoCD good; Flux excellent.
Commercial support: ArgoCD strong (Akuity, others); Flux CNCF community.
Market share (2025): ArgoCD ~60%; Flux ~25%.
Best for: ArgoCD — teams, visibility, onboarding; Flux — platform engineers, automation.

section.p: 2025-2026 Recommendation: ArgoCD for most teams (especially new to GitOps). Flux for platform engineers building automation or with existing Flux deployments.

Other tools in the ecosystem: Kargo (multi-environment promotion, staging to prod), Flagger (automated canary, progressive delivery, works with ArgoCD/Flux), Argo Rollouts (canary/blue-green primitives), Spinnaker (multi-cloud, complex setup).

section.name: gitops-workflow
section.p: Standard GitOps workflow: Developer commits to app repo → CI builds and tests image → CI opens PR against config repo with updated image tag → reviewer approves and merges → GitOps operator (ArgoCD/Flux) detects change → applies to cluster → continuous reconciliation corrects any drift.

rule.statement: Separate CI from CD: build and test in the CI pipeline; deploy exclusively via the GitOps operator reading from the config repo.
rule.rationale: Mixing CI push-deploys with GitOps reconciliation creates two sources of truth. The GitOps operator will revert any push-deployed change not present in Git.

list.item: Phase 1: Assess current state, design target architecture. Document current deployment process, choose tool (ArgoCD vs Flux), define RBAC and secret management strategy.
Phase 2: Basic GitOps setup, single environment. Install operator, connect config repo, migrate one non-critical app, validate sync and drift correction.
Phase 3: Multi-environment, security/compliance integration. Folder-per-environment structure, PR-based promotion, SOPS or External Secrets for secret management, CI validation of manifests.
Phase 4: Multi-cluster management, progressive delivery. Hub-spoke ArgoCD managing multiple clusters, ApplicationSets, Flagger canary deployments.
Phase 5: Self-service developer platforms, advanced automation. Backstage or similar IDP, Kargo multi-stage promotions, AI-powered rollback (Akuity AI).

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use agents to author and review GitOps repos, never to bypass them. Pattern: developer (or agent) opens a PR against the config repo with a Kustomize overlay or Helm values change → CI runs kustomize build / helm template + policy checks (Kyverno/OPA Conftest) + diff preview against current cluster state → human reviews → merge → ArgoCD/Flux reconciles. Agents can scaffold app manifests, run argocd app diff against a candidate branch, and post the diff to the PR. Auto-promotion between envs (dev to staging) happens via PR-bot agents that copy known-good versions; production promotion is human-gated.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent for implementing manifest/Helm chart changes with quality gates and review; custom gitops-pr-author that generates Kustomize overlays / Helm values updates from a structured request; custom gitops-diff-reviewer that runs argocd app diff / flux diff against PR branch and comments on dangerous changes (replica drops, image rollbacks, RBAC widening); custom kargo-promoter that automates Kargo stage promotions with policy checks.

section.name: agent-model-selection
section.p: Agent model routing for GitOps tasks:

list.item: Set up GitHub Actions workflow from template: haiku — pattern application, simple configuration.
Design CI/CD pipeline architecture: opus — complex system design with many variables.
Write terraform code for infrastructure: sonnet — implementation with moderate complexity.
Debug failing pipeline step: sonnet — debugging and problem-solving.
Implement AIOps anomaly detection: opus — novel ML approach, complex decision.
Configure webhook and secret management: haiku — mechanical setup using checklists.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: LLM-specific failure modes when generating GitOps manifests:

list.item: LLMs frequently emit apiVersion: apps/v1beta1 or other deprecated APIs — pin a CRD/API inventory and validate with kubeconform against the target Kubernetes version.
Image tag suggestions sometimes include :latest — explicitly disallow in policies and prompts.
Helm values overrides: agents may set values that the chart does not expose; render and diff before commit.
Multi-line YAML formatting: agents break indentation in nested overlays; always run yamllint and kustomize build on output.
Cross-env contamination: an agent told to "bump api in staging" may also touch prod overlays if path patterns are loose — constrain the working directory in the prompt.
Secret leakage: never let an agent commit a decrypted SOPS file or a kubectl get secret -o yaml dump; route through a password-scrubber-agent.
Auto-merge bots + ArgoCD auto-sync = blast radius; require human approval at least at the prod boundary.

content.prompt:
Analyze my current deployment process and recommend a GitOps adoption strategy.
Current state: - Deployment method: [CI/CD push / manual / scripts] - Infrastructure: [Kubernetes / VMs / serverless] - Team size: [number] - Number of environments: [dev/staging/prod] - Current tools: [Jenkins / GitHub Actions / etc.]
Please provide: 1. Gap analysis between current state and GitOps 2. Recommended GitOps tool (ArgoCD vs Flux) with reasoning 3. Repository structure recommendation 4. Migration phases 5. Risk assessment

Help me decide between push-based and pull-based GitOps for my organization.
Context: - Security requirements: [strict / moderate / basic] - Compliance needs: [SOC2 / HIPAA / PCI-DSS / none] - Multi-cluster: [yes / no, how many clusters] - Team experience with Kubernetes: [expert / intermediate / beginner] - Existing CI/CD: [describe current pipeline]
Provide a recommendation with: 1. Recommended approach (push/pull/hybrid) 2. Security implications 3. Operational complexity comparison 4. Migration effort estimate
